title: Ukraine Drone Strikes Zaporizhzhia Nuclear Plant Radiation Control Lab
summary: A drone struck an external radiation control laboratory at the Russian-controlled Zaporizhzhia nuclear power plant in southeastern Ukraine on May 3. The IAEA warned of safety risks and requested access to assess damage at Europe's largest nuclear facility.
contents:
## Drone Strike on Zaporizhzhia Nuclear Power Plant Lab

### Incident Report
A drone struck an external laboratory that forms part of the Russian-controlled Zaporizhzhia nuclear power plant in southeastern Ukraine on Sunday, May 3. The laboratory handles radiation monitoring and control functions critical to plant safety operations.

### IAEA Response
The International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) warned of safety risks following the strike and submitted a formal request for access to assess damage at the facility. IAEA Director General Rafael Grossi has repeatedly warned about the dangers of military activity near nuclear installations.

### Strategic Context
The Zaporizhzhia nuclear power plant remains Europe's largest nuclear facility by capacity. Russian forces have occupied the plant since early in the Ukraine conflict, and the site has experienced multiple security incidents. The latest strike raises renewed concerns about nuclear safety amid ongoing hostilities.

### Pattern of Escalation
This incident occurred alongside a broader wave of Ukrainian drone attacks across Russia on May 3, including strikes on the Primorsk oil port and shadow fleet tankers. Russia's Defense Ministry reported intercepting 334 Ukrainian UAVs overnight, while Ukraine's Air Force stated Russia attacked with 269 drones and ballistic missiles.

**Sources:** Irish Times, IAEA, Reuters
